3.2 Logistic Regression Model Predicting WNV Presence¶

In [1]:
# Data and module/library loading
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

# Important functionality for this lesson
from scipy import stats
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Setting globally figure size in the notebook
plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (8.0, 6.0)

mosquito_data2_df3 = pd.read_csv(r"C:\Users\raust\OneDrive\Documents\0 BrainStation\BrainStation Data Science\Deliverables\Unit 2 Deliverable 2\mosquito_data_part_2.csv")

With a logistic regression model, the variable to be predicted is categorical.¶

As with the linear regression model, all independent variables will have to be made numeric.¶

In [10]:
# To prepare logistic regression model to predict WNV presence, identify the categorical variables
mosquito_data2_df3.info()

# The relevant independent variables will be Address Block, Trap, Trap type, Date, and Species.

---  ------           --------------  -----

 1   Week             18495 non-null  int64  
 2   Address Block    18495 non-null  object 
 3   Trap             18495 non-null  object 
 4   Trap type        18495 non-null  object 
 5   Date             18495 non-null  object 
 6   Mosquito number  18495 non-null  int64  
 7   WNV Present      18495 non-null  object

 10  Lon              18495 non-null  float64
 11  Month            18495 non-null  int64  
dtypes: float64(2), int64(4), object(6)
memory usage: 1.7+ MB

Preliminary Assessment: Notes Based on Subject Matter Expertise¶

Year: Unless the mosquitos have an n-year cycle, it is not clear how "Year" could be used as a predictive measure for WNV Present.¶
Week: Mosquito prevalence is seasonal because year by year the weeks of the year are consistent with respect to the climatic conditions favourable to mosquito breeding. Still, "Week" is too granular and would add too much complexity and unnecessary computation for the model. "Month" would be a better proxy for mosquito prevalance time frame.¶
Address Block: A categorical variable that will have to be converted to a numeric variable. Still, the only alternatives are that mosquitos were found at the address or they were not. A given address block would be more useful for a heat map showing gradient than for a regression model predicting WNV presence.¶
Trap: Like "Address Block", "Trap ID" is a categorical variable that is more useful for a heat map than a regression model predicting mosquito count.There are too many trap IDs to include in a practical model.¶
Trap Type: A given trap type may have captured more mosquitos, giving some insight into which trap to use more often, but trap type is not a logical predictor of WNV presence.¶
Date: Even more than "Week", the date timestamp down to the second is too granular and computationally intensive for a regression model. Month is the more practical predictor.¶
Mosquito number: The count of mosquitos is a sensible indicator of the likelihood of detecting West Nile Virus.¶
Species: Species is important to know if any harbour the virus more than others.¶
Lat: Without being paired with longitude as a coordinate , latitude is not really a credible predictor. However, since it is known that the test area is limited to the Chicago area, knowing latitude should be one of the better predictors of WNV presence.¶
Lon: Longitude is one of the better predictors of WNV presence for the same reasons as latitude, and shares the same limitation.¶
Month: The most practical predictor of when a virus carried by a seasonal species is most present.¶
In [2]:

Name: WNV Present, dtype: object

Model Refinement¶

While regression models should predict the target as much as possible, they should also be economical. If the same phenomena can be predicted with fewer variables, then it would be more resource-effective to remove the redundant variables.¶

Variable redundancy can be inferred from high collinearity between independent variables of a model. Thus, an important step in model building is to examine the data for multicollinearity via a correlation matrix.¶

In [8]:
# Check for multicollinearity
mosquito_data2_ddf3_X.corr()

In [10]:
# Testing for multicollinearity among the independent variables.
corr_mat = mosquito_data2_ddf3_X.corr()

# Heatmap for visualization
sns.heatmap(corr_mat, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title('Correlation Matrix (Independent Variables)')
plt.show()

Impressions of Sub-Sample 1¶

The 'Date' feature carries data that is too granular. A previous analysis found 'Month' to be a better proxy for time of incidence.¶
The 'Trap' feature is too granular, and is where most of the multicollinearity occurs. 'Trap type' should suffice.¶
The 'Address Block' feature is very granular. However, it provides better location data than latitude and longitude, which need to be considered together as a coordinate to be informative.¶
I will reload the data for clarity of step progression.¶

The Perspective of Subject Matter Expertise¶

An epidemiological study of WNV presence should focus on patterns in location, date-time, and species.¶
Location is important because infectious disease is spread by contact at identifiable locations. To capture location, there is data related to latitude, longitude, and address block. Trap (Trap ID) may be a proxy for location if the coordinates of the individual units are readily known. I argue that Address Block can serve as the sole proxy for location.¶
For the date-time factor, there are a number of features: Year, Month, Week, and Date (timestamp). WNV is being spread by mosquitos, whose appearance is seasonal (i.e. summer months). While some years may have higher incidence than others, using Year in the model is of questionable value for predictive purposes, unless some relationship can be made between multiyear cycles (e.g. prime number years). That is not the case with WNV. Weeks may be informative, but intuitively, the weeks of interest should correlate highly with the months of interest. Weeks is too granular to be practical in the model. Date, capturing the precise time stamp down to the second, is even more granular and so less practical for a regression model. Date-Time could/should arguably be captured by "Month" alone.¶
Species is important because some species may harbour the virus more than others.¶
In [3]:
# Reload the data then remove the 'Date', 'Trap', 'Lat', 'Lon' features before recreating the dummy variables.

# Data and module/library loading
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

# Important functionality for this lesson
from scipy import stats
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Setting globally figure size in the notebook
plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (8.0, 6.0)

mosquito_data2_df3 = pd.read_csv(r"C:\Users\raust\OneDrive\Documents\0 BrainStation\BrainStation Data Science\Deliverables\Unit 2 Deliverable 2\mosquito_data_part_2.csv")

# Prepare to convert categorical (i.e. object) fields to numerical ones.
mosquito_data2_df3b = mosquito_data2_df3.copy()

# Drop the problematic columns
mosquito_data2_df3c = mosquito_data2_df3b.drop(['Year', 'Week', 'Date', 'Trap', 'Lat', 'Lon'], axis=1)

from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder

categories_to_make_numerical = mosquito_data2_df3c.select_dtypes(include='object').columns.tolist()

mosquito_data2_ddf3 = pd.get_dummies(mosquito_data2_df3c, columns=categories_to_make_numerical, drop_first=True)


mosquito_data2_ddf3.head() # Check

Impressions of Sub-Sample 2¶

The above result is not satisfactory either, as there are too many address blocks for the model to be practical.¶

Next, I will try to determine the address blocks with the highest correlation to the target variable, and only keep those.¶

I will again re-import and prepare the data for clarity of steps.¶

In [11]:
# Data and module/library loading
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

# Important functionality for this lesson
from scipy import stats
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Setting globally figure size in the notebook
plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (8.0, 6.0)

mosquito_data2_df3 = pd.read_csv(r"C:\Users\raust\OneDrive\Documents\0 BrainStation\BrainStation Data Science\Deliverables\Unit 2 Deliverable 2\mosquito_data_part_2.csv")

# Prepare to convert categorical (i.e. object) fields to numerical ones.
mosquito_data2_df3b = mosquito_data2_df3.copy()

# Convert Address Block and WNV Present to dummy variables to see which blocks correlate highly with WNV Present
address_WNVpresent_df = pd.DataFrame(mosquito_data2_df3b, columns=['Address Block', 'WNV Present'])
#address_WNVpresent_df.head()

address_WNVpresent_ddf = pd.get_dummies(address_WNVpresent_df)
address_WNVpresent_ddf.head()

# Find correlations between Address Block and WNV Present
top_corr_addr_WNVneg = address_WNVpresent_ddf.corr()["WNV Present_negative"].nlargest(20)
print(top_corr_addr_WNVneg)
print("")
top_corr_addr_WNVpos = address_WNVpresent_ddf.corr()["WNV Present_positive"].nlargest(20)
print(top_corr_addr_WNVpos)

No address block appeared among the top 20 for both positive and negative WNV presence.¶

Ohare Airport had double the correlation (and a positive presence correlation) of any other address block. The address block for Ohare Airport will be used as the proxy for "location feature". Including any others would increase the complexity of the model, with questionable benefit.¶

A scatterplot mapping WNV presence by latitude and longitude would be informative. It may roughly show the geographic shape of the Chicagoland test area.¶

In [12]:
# Prepare independent variables
# remove WNV Present
mosquito_data2_ddf3_X = pd.DataFrame(mosquito_data2_ddf3, columns=['Mosquito number', 'Month', 'Address Block_100XX W OHARE AIRPORT', 'Trap type_GRAVID', 'Trap type_OVI', 'Trap type_SENTINEL', 'Species_CULEX RESTUANS', 'Species_CULEX SALINARIUS', 'Species_CULEX TERRITANS'])
mosquito_data2_ddf3_X.info()

# Add a constant for the intercept
mosquito_data2_ddf3_X_wconst = sm.add_constant(mosquito_data2_ddf3_X)
mosquito_data2_ddf3_X_wconst.info()

# Prepare dependent variable
mosquito_data2_df3b_y = mosquito_data2_df3b['WNV Present']
mosquito_data2_df3b_y.head()

In [39]:
# Use logit function from statsmodels to fit the logistic regression model 

X = mosquito_data2_ddf3_X.replace({True: 1, False: 0})
X_wconst = sm.add_constant(X)

y = mosquito_data2_df3b_y.replace({'positive': 1, 'negative': 0})

# 1. Instantiate model
logreg_wnv_present = sm.Logit(y, X_wconst)

# 2. Fit the model (this returns a separate object with the parameters)
logreg_results = logreg_wnv_present.fit()

logreg_results.summary()

Warning: Maximum number of iterations has been exceeded.
         Current function value: 0.419578
         Iterations: 35

C:\Users\raust\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\statsmodels\base\model.py:607: ConvergenceWarning: Maximum Likelihood optimization failed to converge. Check mle_retvals
  warnings.warn("Maximum Likelihood optimization failed to "

Model Issues¶

1. I had to replace "True" and "False" entries with "1" and "0" for the model to run. Likewise for "Positive" and "Negative" entries. Otherwise, the program rejected the data with "ValueError: Pandas data to numpy dtype of object. Check input data with np.asarray(data)."¶
2. There was also a convergence warning: "Maximum Likelihood optimization failed to converge. Check mle_retvals".¶
3. Finally, there was a related warning stating that the maximum number of iterations had been exceeded. I do not know what to do in these situations. The model still produced an output summary (see below).¶
In [38]:

logreg_results.summary()

Interpreting the Results¶

1. The constant term was negligible, meaning there was essentially no bias that needs to be added.¶
2. With each unit increase in the number of mosquitos caught, the odds of having a positive WNV reading go up by 1.0699. That relationship in general should be intuitive.¶
3. Being at the block at OHARE Airport increases the odds of a positive WNV reading by 1.722, easily the place most likely to get a postive reading.¶
4. Using the GRAVID or OVI trap types increased the odds of a positive WNV reading by more than the increase when using the OVI trap type. The impact of using the CDC trap type is not apparent from the model, although there should be a linear relationship between using CDC and not using the others.¶
5. From the model, catching a mosquito of the type C Restuans species meant a much higher odds of finding a WNV-infected mosquito than catching C Salinarius or C Territans . As with the CDC trap type, the impact of C Pipiens was not clear from the model.¶

Conclusions¶

This model should NOT be used for WNV presence prediction.¶
The features correlate poorly with the target variable.¶
More data from relevant features is needed.¶
Data is needed to test the accuracy of the model.¶
